skill.name: developing-genkit-go
skill.description: Develop AI-powered applications in Go with Firebase Genkit. Use this skill for Genkit flows, prompts, streaming, tool calling, structured output, and model provider setup.
skill.summary: Build Go AI features with Firebase Genkit flows, prompts, tools, streaming, and providers.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from markdown fences, Go import statements, localhost examples, schema tags, and provider documentation. The real concern is repeated documentation that installs the Genkit CLI with a remote script piped directly to bash. No evidence found of malicious intent, credential exfiltration, prompt injection, or code that executes during skill loading.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: The skill documents installing the Genkit CLI with curl piped directly to bash. This is risky because it encourages executing remote code without integrity checks or review, even though the skill does not run it automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command appears in installation instructions. It is a documentation risk, not confirmed malicious runtime behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts are markdown backticks around Go, bash, and inline API examples. They are reference text and are not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown examples and inline formatting. No executable skill script or automatic command runner is present.

low_findings.title: Provider Credential Names Without Secret Values
low_findings.description: The provider reference names environment variables such as API key names. It does not include real secret values or transmit credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows placeholder environment variable names and os.Getenv usage. No hardcoded credential or exfiltration endpoint is present.

low_findings.title: Local Development Network Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs and loopback address are local development examples for Genkit handlers, Developer UI, telemetry, and Ollama. They do not indicate external data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators point to 127.0.0.1 and localhost services for local development. No remote collection endpoint is shown.

low_findings.title: Prompt Syntax Alerts Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: System-role and output-instruction examples are normal Genkit prompt features. No evidence found of instructions that target the evaluator or ask to bypass review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines demonstrate application prompt roles and output formatting. They do not contain marketplace review bypass language or evaluator instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote shell installation guidance should be replaced or supplemented with verified package-manager or checksum-based instructions before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both locations show the same remote-install pattern. The pattern is dangerous as user guidance even though it is not automatically executed.

content.value_statement: Building AI features in Go requires correct Genkit setup, flow patterns, prompts, tools, and provider configuration. This skill gives focused guidance and examples for implementing Genkit Go applications.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to initialize Genkit Go with Google AI, Vertex AI, Anthropic, OpenAI-compatible, and Ollama providers.
Shows how to define flows, streaming flows, HTTP handlers, and context providers.
Guides text generation, structured output, streaming, model configuration, and output formats.
Documents code-defined prompts, dotprompt files, schemas, roles, media prompts, conditionals, and loops.
Covers tool definition, tool choice, max turns, tool interrupts, restarts, and direct tool responses.
Lists Genkit CLI commands for local development, flow testing, tracing, and documentation lookup.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not a runnable Genkit project or code generator package.
Model names can change, so provider documentation should be checked before production use.
The examples assume the user supplies valid API keys, cloud credentials, and Go project configuration.
Security, authentication, deployment, and production operations require additional project-specific review.

use_cases.title: Create a Genkit Flow
use_cases.description: Build a Go flow that wraps generation logic, can be tested locally, and can be exposed through an HTTP handler.

use_cases.title: Design Reusable Prompts
use_cases.description: Move prompt logic into code-defined prompts or dotprompt files with schemas, roles, and typed inputs.

use_cases.title: Connect Model Providers
use_cases.description: Configure Genkit with Google AI, Vertex AI, Anthropic Claude, OpenAI-compatible APIs, or local Ollama models.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Genkit Go Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a new Genkit Go project with Google AI. Include setup steps, initialization, and a simple flow.

prompt_templates.title: Add Structured Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to return typed structured output from a Genkit Go generation call using a Go struct and schema descriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Build Tool Calling
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Genkit Go tool calling flow for this use case: [describe use case]. Include tool definitions and max-turn guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Genkit Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Genkit Go architecture for flows, prompts, providers, streaming, tool interrupts, and deployment concerns: [paste design].

output_examples.input: User asks for a first Genkit Go flow with Google AI.
output: A short setup sequence for Go modules and Genkit dependencies.
A flow design that passes the Genkit instance explicitly.
Guidance for testing the flow with the local Developer UI.

output_examples.input: User asks how to add a typed prompt file.
output: A dotprompt structure with frontmatter, input schema, and output schema.
Instructions to register matching Go types before lookup.
Notes about when to choose dotprompt files instead of inline prompts.

output_examples.input: User asks which provider plugin to use for Claude.
output: Provider guidance for the Anthropic Genkit plugin.
The required environment variable name without exposing any secret value.
A reminder to verify current model identifiers in provider documentation.

best_practices: Pass the Genkit instance explicitly through your code instead of storing it as a global variable.
Wrap reusable AI logic in flows so it can be traced, tested, and served consistently.
Use typed schemas and clear tool descriptions so models receive precise structure and intent.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode API keys, provider credentials, or production secrets in examples or application code.
Do not expose development flow handlers on public interfaces without authentication and authorization.
Do not pipe remote install scripts into a shell without verification and source review.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Go applications that use Firebase Genkit for generation, flows, prompts, tools, streaming, and model providers.

faq.question: Does it support Claude models?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Anthropic provider guidance and mentions the required environment variable for Claude API access.

faq.question: Can it create HTTP endpoints for flows?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents Genkit HTTP handlers, streaming handlers, request formats, and request context providers.

faq.question: Does it include production deployment guidance?
faq.answer: Only basic serving patterns are covered. Production security, authentication, scaling, and operations need separate review.

faq.question: Are current model names guaranteed?
faq.answer: No. The skill advises users to check provider documentation because model identifiers change over time.

faq.question: Can it help with tool interrupts?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains interrupt handling, restarts, direct responses, and resume-state checks for Genkit tools.
